coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence-infrared absorption dual-mode nanoprobes based on carbon dots@SiO2 nanorods for ultrasensitive and reliable detection of carcinoembryonic antigen
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Kexin
Ding, Yadan
Yang, Weiqiang
Wen, Xiaokun
Zhao, Huiying
Liu, Yanmei
Hong, Xia
coredata.dc:description: The level of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) in serum has the significant reference value for early diagnosis and treatment of various cancers. However, the CEA detection still suffers from the issue of limited sensitivity and reliability. Herein, a fluorescence (FL)-infrared absorption (IRA) dual-mode nanoprobe was fabricated based on carbon dots (CDs)@SiO2 nanorod for CEA detection. The FL and IRA signals display no mutual interference and can verify each other, ensuring the reliability of assay results. The highly sensitive FL signal originating from the CDs is enhanced by the surface passivation of SiO2 and improves the overall sensitivity of the detection. The detection range spans 9 orders of magnitude and the limit of detection reaches 794.6 ag mL−1, which are great superior to the commercial kits and most of the previous reports. Satisfactory recovery over the commercial kits was achieved in real serum samples. The ultrasensitive and reliable FL-IRA detection strategy sheds light on a new avenue toward promoting the practicability of the nanoprobes in clinical cancer diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoprobe
Carbon dot
Silica
Immunoassay
Cancer biomarker

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Amino functionalization of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods
Preparation of the dual-mode immunoassay nanoprobes
Preparation of the sandwich immunoassay substrates
Assay of the real serum samples
FL-IRA detection based on CDs@SiO2 nanorods
Application in human serum samples detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CDs. The inset of (a) is the HRTEM image of the CDs. (b) AFM image of the CDs and the height profile along the white line drawn in the AFM image. (c) X-ray diffraction pattern of the CDs. (d) SEM image of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs, SiO2 nanorods and the CDs@SiO2 nanorods. (b) FL spectra of the CDs, SiO2 nanorods and the CDs@SiO2 nanorods. The excitation wavelength is 360 nm. The inset is the visualized presentation of the water dispersion of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods under the UV irradiation with a central wavelength of about 360 nm. (c) FL spectra of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods obtained under different excitation wavelengths (260 nm–460 nm). (d) FTIR absorption spectra of the CDs, SiO2 nanorods and the CDs@SiO2 nanorods.

(a) Preparation process of the nanoprobe/analyte complexes. The CDs@SiO2 nanorods were functionalized by anti-CEA and the residual sites were blocked by BSA to fabricate the dual-mode nanoprobe. The analyte CEA was then captured by the nanoprobes. (b) Schematic illustration of the sandwich immunoassay protocol. The nanoprobe/analyte complexes were incubated with the substrates modified with anti-CEA.
Effect of (a) temperature and (b) time on the assay results in the incubation process of the nanoprobe/analyte complexes and the substrate.

(a) FL spectra of the sandwich immunoassay with different analyte concentrations. The excitation wavelength is 380 nm. (b) Calibration curve: linear fitting by the logarithm of CEA concentration (1 fg mL−1 – 10 ng mL−1) and the FL intensity at 449 nm. (c) IRA spectra of the sandwich immunoassay with different analyte concentrations. (d) Calibration curve: linear fitting by the logarithm of PSA concentration (2 pg mL−1 – 200 ng mL−1) and the IRA at 1099 cm−1. Error bars in (b) and (d) indicate the standard deviations from the mean of 5 parallel results.

Specificity and selectivity of the dual-mode nanoprobes. (a) The FL intensities at 449 nm and (b) the IRA at 1099 cm−1 obtained in the coexisting of the analyte (100 pg mL−1) and the interfering agents (10 μg mL−1) or only the existing of the interfering agents. The interfering agents included PSA, AFP, HIgG, HIgM, HIgA, UA and GLU. Error bars indicate the standard deviations from the mean of 5 parallel results. The blank result was obtained without addition of the analyte CEA in the assay process.
A comparison of assay performance on recently reported nanomaterial-based method for CEA.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: PEC: photoelectrochemistry; EC: electrochemical; CNT: carbon nanotube; PAMAM: poly(amidoamine); GO: graphene.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection results of CEA in real serum samples.

row.entry: Measurements of real serum samples (ng mL−1)

head.ce:title: Fluorescence-infrared absorption dual-mode nanoprobes based on carbon dots@SiO2 nanorods for ultrasensitive and reliable detection of carcinoembryonic antigen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of UV-Emitting Materials and Technology (Northeast Normal University), Ministry of Education, Changchun, 130024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of UV-Emitting Materials and Technology (Northeast Normal University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of UV-Emitting Materials and Technology (Northeast Normal University), Ministry of Education, Changchun 130024, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Geriatrics, First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Geriatrics, First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130021, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The level of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) in serum has the significant reference value for early diagnosis and treatment of various cancers. However, the CEA detection still suffers from the issue of limited sensitivity and reliability. Herein, a fluorescence (FL)-infrared absorption (IRA) dual-mode nanoprobe was fabricated based on carbon dots (CDs)@SiO2 nanorod for CEA detection. The FL and IRA signals display no mutual interference and can verify each other, ensuring the reliability of assay results. The highly sensitive FL signal originating from the CDs is enhanced by the surface passivation of SiO2 and improves the overall sensitivity of the detection. The detection range spans 9 orders of magnitude and the limit of detection reaches 794.6 ag mL−1, which are great superior to the commercial kits and most of the previous reports. Satisfactory recovery over the commercial kits was achieved in real serum samples. The ultrasensitive and reliable FL-IRA detection strategy sheds light on a new avenue toward promoting the practicability of the nanoprobes in clinical cancer diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Kexin Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Weiqiang Yang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Yadan Ding: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft. Xiaokun Wen: Writing - review & editing, Huiying Zhao: Validation, Funding acquisition. Yanmei Liu: Validation, Investigation. Xia Hong: Supervision, Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

Cancer is one of the major worldwide public health threats for all mankind [1,2]. The low cure rate of cancer patients in the middle and late stage forces people to attach great importance on the early diagnosis of cancer [3]. The determination of cancer marker level is an efficient and economical method of early diagnosis, especially in resource constrained regions and countries [4]. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is one of the widely used cancer marker in clinic [5,6]. It is an indispensable reference for the screening and diagnosis of lung cancer, colorectal cancer, esophageal cancer, gastric carcinoma and pancreatic carcinoma [7–9]. The safe concentration of CEA in human serum is below 5 ng mL−1. The CEA detection is considerably essential for preventing the risk of cancer, but the clinically false-positive results may cause the misdiagnosis of doctors, psychological distress of patients and increase of health care costs. Therefore, the accurate and sensitive CEA detection is of great significance for the early diagnosis and treatment of cancers.

The rapidly developing nanotechnology invigorates the clinical detection for CEA [10–14]. The diversified nanoprobes derive a series of promising immunoassay methods, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), electrochemistry, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and colorimetry [15–17]. They are particularly attractive for the detection at low levels toward CEA in the early diagnosis of cancers. Despite their distinctive advantages, there are currently some regrets in these methods including the interference of the background signal, the photobleaching of organic dyes and the pollution of electrolyte solution. The developing of reliable and sensitive nanoprobes is still indispensable.

SiO2 as a classic nanomaterial has been widespread used in biodetection field [18]. But it usually serves as the inert protective layer and coupling agent rather than as the nanoprobe [19–21]. Actually, SiO2 has a unique property that is always ignored. Its asymmetric stretching of Si–O–Si bonds offers a pair of the transverse optical (TO) and longitudinal optical (LO) phonon modes in infrared absorption (IRA) spectrum, which are considered as the fingerprint IRA of SiO2 [22]. Our previous works have demonstrated that the SiO2 nanoparticles can be used as the nanoprobes for protein detection [23,24]. The fingerprint IRA from Si–O–Si bonds of SiO2 was used as the detection signal and showed an outstanding anti-interference capability, revealing the promising and charming future to decrease and even avoid the false-positive results. However, the limited sensitivity restricts the clinical applications of the IRA nanoprobes based on SiO2. It is difficult to further improve the sensitivity on totally their own. Introduction of another sensitive detection signal to construct the dual-mode nanoprobes offers a promising alternative to address the issue and even improve some significant detection index [25,26].

The high sensitivity of fluorescence (FL) nanoprobes is universally acknowledged [27–30]. Up to now, the FL nanoprobe has become one of the most effective analysis tools to achieve the ultrasensitive protein detection [31–33]. Among them, carbon dot (CD) is favored by researchers because of its high FL quantum yields, stable emission without the effect from photobleaching, nontoxicity and good chemical stability [34–36]. The combination of CDs and SiO2 has the potential to improve overall detection sensitivity, linear range and reliability compared with those of single-mode nanoprobes due to enhanced fluorescence derived from surface passivation of SiO2, complementarity and mutual verification between IRA and FL signals, thus shedding light on a new avenue toward promoting the clinical applications of SiO2-based nanoprobes.

In this work, CDs@SiO2 nanorods were prepared and biofunctionalized to serve as a FL-IRA dual-mode nanoprobe for CEA detection. Whether the crosstalk exists between the IRA signal of SiO2 component and the FL signal of CDs was studied. The enhancement of the FL performance of CDs was verified. The assay performances including sensitivity, detection range, selectivity and specificity were evaluated. Moreover, CEA in real serum samples was detected to verify the application value of the nanoprobes in the clinical diagnosis.
The source and purity of the reagents used in this work are recorded in Supporting Information (SI).

Anhydrous citric acid (4.00 g) and carbamide (6.50 g) were dissolved in deionized water (20 mL). The mixed solution was sealed into the reaction kettle and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the reaction solution was centrifuged to remove the precipitates. The supernatant was dialyzed by deionized water (1 L) for 48 h. The dialyzed solution was concentrated to 50 mL by the rotary evaporator. The concentrated solution was frozen by liquid nitrogen and lyophilized to obtain the powder products. The powder was stored in the dry and dark environment for further use.

The CDs@SiO2 nanorods were synthesized by a modified microemulsion system. Briefly, the preformed CDs were dispersed in a sodium citrate solution (25.7 mM, 0.4 mL). The ammonium hydroxide (28–30%, 0.3 mL) was dropped in the dispersion to adjust the pH value to 12.6. The dispersion was evenly mixed with a 1-pentanol solution of PVP (0.2 g mL−1, 10 mL) and ethanol (1 mL) by vortex. Then tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, 100 μL) was added to the mixture and mixed by vortex again. The mixture was placed at 60 °C for 2 h. The products were centrifuged and washed successively with ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amino functionalization of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods
ce:section.ce:para: The preformed CDs@SiO2 nanorods were dispersed in ethanol (15 mL). Deionized water (10 μL) and (3-aminopropyl) -trimethoxysilane (APTES, 5 μL) were then added to the dispersion. The mixture was kept in stirring at 60 °C for 6 h. The pale-yellow precipitates were centrifuged and washed by deionized water three times. The products were finally dispersed in deionized water (2 mL) for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the dual-mode immunoassay nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: The dispersion of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods (2.5 mg mL−1, 2 mL) was mixed with glutaraldehyde (25 vol%, 3 mL). The mixture was shaken gently at room temperature for 4 h. After centrifuging and washing with phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.01 M, pH = 7.2), the precipitates were redispersed in PBS (4 mL). An anti-CEA solution (20 μg mL−1, 1 mL) was added into the dispersion for 2 h at 37 °C and kept slight shaking. Then a bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution (1 mg mL−1, 40 μL) was added for blocking the residual active sites. The excess antibodies and BSA were removed by low-speed centrifugation and washing with PBS. The antibody-functionalized nanorods were suspended in PBS. The CEA solution (1 mL) with definite concentration (200 ng mL−1 – 500 fg mL−1) was incubated with the antibody-functionalized nanorods at 37 °C for 2 h. The analytes/nanoprobe composites were dispersed in PBS (2 mL) after centrifugation and washing operations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the sandwich immunoassay substrates
ce:section.ce:para: The monocrystalline silicon wafers were ultrasonically washed by deionized water and then immersed in hydrofluoric acid for 3 h. After washing by deionized water, an Au film was evaporated on the silicon wafers by vacuum sputtering. Then the Au-plated silicon wafers were immersed in the thioctic acid ethanol solution (0.02 g mL−1) at room temperature for 4 h, after which the wafers were thoroughly washed by ethanol and dried by nitrogen blowing. The 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were used to crosslink the anti-CEA and the silicon wafer to obtain the immunoassay substrates. The wafers were soaked in PBS of the EDC/NHS (0.1 M/0.02 M) at room temperature with gentle shaking for 1 h. After washing with PBS and drying with nitrogen, the silicon wafers were incubated with the anti-CEA solution (10 μg mL−1, 5 mL) at 37 °C for 2 h with gentle shaking. A BSA solution (1 mg mL−1) was then dropped to block the residual active sites with lightly shaking for 1 h. The obtained immunoassay substrates were gently washed by PBS and finally maintained in PBS at 4 °C.

The dispersion of the analytes/nanoprobe complexes (2 mL) was incubated with the immunoassay substrates under gentle shaking at 37 °C for 2 h. The immunoassay substrates were gently washed with PBS to remove the unspecific adsorptive nanoprobes. Finally, the FL and IRA spectra of the immunoassay substrates were characterized and recorded.

The specificity of the immunoassay method was tested by replacing the analyte CEA with the interferents. The interferents included prostate specific antigen (PSA), α-fetoprotein (AFP), human immunoglobulin G (HIgG), human immunoglobulin M (HIgM) and uric acid (UA). The concentration of the interferents is 10 μg mL−1. The selectivity experiment was carried out by testing the response of nanoprobes in the mixture. The mixture (1 mL) consisted of the CEA (100 pg mL−1) and the interferent. Other operation steps of the dual-mode immunoassay were identical with section 2.7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of the real serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The whole blood samples were collected from the anonymous volunteers in the Affiliated Hospital of Northeast Normal University and coagulated at 20 °C for 2 h. Then they were condensed at 4 °C for 8 h to separate the serum naturally. The serums were obtained and further purified by centrifugation at 4 °C. Before the spiking, the serum was detected by the commercial ELISA kit for CEA. The average of the results from 3 parallel tests was taken as the CEA concentration of the sample. The CEA concentration of the serum sample was determined to be 1.04 ng mL−1. According to the standard addition method, the serum was spiked with specific CEA concentrations (10 pg mL−1, 100 pg mL−1, 1 ng mL−1 and 10 ng mL−1). The rest procedures of the immunoassay were identical to those in section 2.5, 2.6 and 2.7.
The relevant characterization instruments and details in this work were described in SI.

The coating of SiO2 on the CDs was carried out in a microemulsion system by hydrolysis and condensation of TEOS. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image shows that the CDs have an average size of 6.1 ± 1.6 nm (Fig. 1 a). The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image exhibits lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 1a). It matches with the (002) crystal face of the graphite reported in previous studies [37,38]. The CDs are 6.4 ± 1.0 nm in diameter and 5.9 ± 0.4 nm in height measured by the atomic force microscopy (Fig. 1b). They are consistent with the size determined from the TEM image. Fig. 1c shows the X-ray diffraction pattern of the CDs. A broad band centered at about 25.6° coincides with the (002) plane of the graphite carbon structure. It is consistent with the result of HRTEM image. The CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites have the rod-like morphology (Fig. 1d). The average length and diameter are 1.1 ± 0.1 μm and 364.0 ± 36.1 nm, respectively.

The optical properties of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods were investigated using the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, FL spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The CDs and the SiO2 nanorods were used as the control sample. The CDs exhibit characteristic absorption at 246 and 340 nm (Fig. 2 a), which are respectively ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains of the CC bonds and the n-π* transition of the CO bonds. The CDs@SiO2 nanorods combine the absorption peaks from the CDs and SiO2.

The FL spectra of the CDs, SiO2 nanorods and CDs@SiO2 nanorods were obtained under the excited light with the wavelength of 360 nm. As shown in Fig. 2b, the maximum emission intensity of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods appears at 443 nm. Compared with the CDs, there are no remarkable changes in the emission peak position, but the FL intensity increases by about 3.4 times. The SiO2 nanorods prepared by the same microemulsion method have no FL property under the same excited light. Therefore, the improvement of the FL performance is believed to originate from the surface passivation of the SiO2 shell. The dispersion of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods is milky and emits blue light under the UV irradiation (inset of Fig. 2b). It fits well with the result of the FL spectrum. According to the reported works, the CDs typically have the excitation-dependent emission behavior [34,39]. The CDs@SiO2 nanorods retain this property (Fig. 2c). When the wavelength of excitation light changes from 260 nm to 460 nm, the maximal emission peak position of the CDs@SiO2 nanorods moves from 438 nm to 525 nm. The maximum emission intensity is obtained when the wavelength of the excitation light is 380 nm. The FL microscope was used to observe the CDs@SiO2 nanorods under excitation with different wavelength. The CDs@SiO2 nanorods emit blue light, green light and red light under the irradiation of UV, blue and green light, respectively (Fig. S1). The above results indicate that the coating of SiO2 improved the FL performance of the CDs. It facilitates promoting the sensitivity of the detection.

As shown in the IRA spectrum, the CDs@SiO2 nanorods obtained the fingerprint IRA from the SiO2 component (Fig. 2d). It centers at 1099 cm−1 and has a shoulder at 1195 cm−1. The band arises from the TO-LO phonon mode of the antisymmetric stretching vibration of the Si–O–Si bonds and always appears in pair [23]. It has the strong capability to resist the interference. The IRA of CDs component almost has no effect on the infrared fingerprint of SiO2. The non-interference FL and IRA signals can verify each other, ensuring the accuracy of dual-mode detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL-IRA detection based on CDs@SiO2 nanorods
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the preparation steps of the dual-mode nanoprobes and the fabrication process of the sandwich detection strategy. The anti-CEA was first assembled on the CDs@SiO2 nanorods and blocked with BSA to obtain the nanoprobes. Then the nanoprobes captured the analyte CEA, composing analytes/nanoprobe complexes. Meanwhile, the anti-CEA was also immobilized on a silicon wafer with plating gold to form a substrate. After blocking with BSA, the substrate and the nanoprobe/analyte complexes completed the recognition and coupling. The assay results were output by testing the FL and IRA spectra of the substrates.

The detection conditions such as the wavelength of the excitation source, the incubation temperature and time of the nanoprobe/analyte complexes and the substrate have the significant effect on the assay performance. Therefore, the optimized experiments were designed. The analyte CEA concentration was fixed at 1 ng mL−1 in optimized experiments. The wavelength of excitation was determined to be 380 nm according to Fig. 2c. The FL intensity at 449 nm and the absorbance at 1099 cm−1 were gained for the quantitative analysis of CEA. As shown in Fig. 4 , the two signal intensity both reached the maximum at 37 °C for 1 h incubation. Thus, the incubation temperature and time of the nanoprobe/analyte complexes with the substrate were determined as 37 °C and 1 h.

The specificity and selectivity are important indicators to evaluate the anti-interference capability of the dual-mode nanoprobes. The experiments to determine the specificity and selectivity were conducted with the existence of interfering species including analogous proteins (PSA, AFP, HIgG, HIgM and HIgA) and the common small biomolecule (UA) and glucose (GLU) with a high concentration (10 μg mL−1). In order to verify the specificity of the nanoprobes, the single interfering species replaced CEA to be captured by the nanoprobes. A blank experiment with no analyte CEA adding was set as a control. The response of the nanoprobes to the single interfering species was almost identical with that of the blank control (Fig. 6 ). It demonstrated that the nanoprobes had favorable specificity. The selectivity of the dual-mode nanoprobes for CEA was then investigated. The interfering species (PSA, AFP, HIgG, HIgM, HIgA, UA and GLU) were mixed with CEA respectively in the incubating process of the nanoprobes and the analyte. The response of the nanoprobes for these mixtures was characterized. The FL and IRA readouts were almost consistent with the results of CEA only, indicating an acceptable selectivity for CEA detection in the presence of the interfering species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in human serum samples detection
ce:section.ce:para: The CEA detection in human serum samples was carried out to examine the practical application value. The immunoassay results were recorded in Table 2 . The recoveries ranged from 98.3% to 106.6%, which is comparable or superior to those of the commercial ELISA kits, such as Abcam Co., Ltd (92%–116%), Shanghai Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd (94%–102%) and Elabscience Biotechnology Co., Ltd (93%–104%). The dual-mode nanoprobes were expected to find application in the in vitro detection of the cancer biomarker.

The FL-IRA dual-mode nanoprobes were successfully fabricated based on the biofunctionalized CDs@SiO2 nanorods. CDs component introduced ultrasensitive FL signal, which was enhanced by the surface passivation of SiO2. The FL and IRA signal can verify mutually to ensure the reliability of detection. The overall sensitivity of the nanoprobes was improved. Compared to the commercial ELISA Kits, the dual-mode detection method based on the FL-IRA nanoprobes showed higher sensitivity and comparable recovery in serum CEA detection. The method can be easily adapted to detect other proteins, DNA or RNA. The application of the dual-mode nanoprobes for on-field detection is expected to be achieved in future works based on the portable spectrometers.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 51972052, 11604043 and 51772122), Scientific and Technological Developing Scheme of Jilin Province (No. 20190201243JC), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2412019FZ033) and the 111 Project (No. B13013).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel colorimetric enzyme immunoassay for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient streptavidin-functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene for the development of highly sensitive electrochemical immunosensor
